perturbation.original_text:
Therefore, in consideration of the covenants of all Financial Institutions contained herein, Employee agrees that during Employee’s employment with a Financial Institution, and for a period of one (1) year immediately following the termination of Employee’s employment with a Financial Institution (“Restriction Period”), for whatever reason and regardless of how Employee’s employment was terminated, Employee shall not, directly or indirectly (including, but not limited to, through other individuals, any partnership, corporation or business entity in which Employee has an ownership interest or serves as an officer, employee, independent contractor, representative, agent or consultant), either for Employee’s own benefit or the benefit of any other Person:
a. Solicit, divert, or take away (or attempt to Solicit, divert or take away) any Banking and Financial Services to or from any Customer or Potential Customer; or b. advise, persuade or induce (or attempt to advise, persuade or induce) any Customer or Potential Customer to terminate, reduce, limit, or change the Customer’s services or business relationship, or the Potential Customer’s contemplated services or business relationship, with a Financial Institution; or c. directly provide Banking and Financial Services to, or directly accept Banking and Financial Services from, any Customer or Potential Customer.
perturbation.changed_text:
Therefore, in consideration of the covenants of all Financial Institutions contained herein, Employee agrees that during Employee’s employment with a Financial Institution, Employee shall not, directly or indirectly (including, but not limited to, through other individuals, any partnership, corporation or business entity in which Employee has an ownership interest or serves as an officer, employee, independent contractor, representative, agent or consultant), either for Employee’s own benefit or the benefit of any other Person:
a. Solicit, divert, or take away (or attempt to Solicit, divert or take away) any Banking and Financial Services to or from any Customer or Potential Customer; or b. advise, persuade or induce (or attempt to advise, persuade or induce) any Customer or Potential Customer to terminate, reduce, limit, or change the Customer’s services or business relationship, or the Potential Customer’s contemplated services or business relationship, with a Financial Institution; or c. directly provide Banking and Financial Services to, or directly accept Banking and Financial Services from, any Customer or Potential Customer.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the one-year non-solicitation clause after termination of employment weakens the protection of the company's customer base. Under Indiana law, non-compete agreements are enforceable if they are reasonable in scope and duration. Removing the duration entirely creates a potential issue as it could be argued the restriction is now unreasonable and thus unenforceable.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text removes the temporal restriction on the non-solicitation agreement, potentially making it unenforceable under Indiana law because Indiana courts require non-compete agreements to be reasonable in scope and duration. By removing the specified one-year restriction period, the agreement is likely too broad and therefore contradicts the spirit and requirements of Indiana law regarding reasonable restrictions on competition.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Indiana General Assembly You need to enable JavaScript to run this app.
perturbation.justification: The changed text removes the one-year restriction on the non-solicitation agreement after the termination of employment. The Perturbation and Law Explanations state that under Indiana law, non-compete agreements must be reasonable in scope and duration to be enforceable. By removing the specified one-year restriction, the agreement becomes too broad, potentially contradicting the requirements of Indiana law. The Scraped Law Snippet 1 mentions 'Indiana General Assembly' which indicates this contract is subject to Indiana state law. While Scraped Law Snippet 2 is 'None', the Perturbation and Law Explanations, combined with the first snippet, suggest that removing the temporal restriction likely makes the non-compete clause unenforceable under Indiana law.

perturbation.original_text: Employee agrees that all Confidential Information and all records, documents and materials relating to all Confidential Information (including all copies thereof) shall be and remain the sole and exclusive property of a Banking Organization, and upon Employee’s termination of employment, all such Confidential Information, whether in paper or electronic form, shall be returned to a Financial Institution in good condition, without Employee retaining a copy thereof, including, but not limited to, retaining an electronic copy on any electronic device regardless of whether such device is owned by Employee. At a Financial Institution’s request, Employee shall provide written verification, under the penalties for perjury, of his/her compliance with this covenant.
perturbation.changed_text: Employee agrees that all Confidential Information and all records, documents and materials relating to all Confidential Information (including all copies thereof) shall be and remain the sole and exclusive property of a Banking Organization, and upon Employee’s termination of employment, all such Confidential Information, whether in paper or electronic form, shall be returned to a Financial Institution in good condition, without Employee retaining a copy thereof. At a Financial Institution’s request, Employee shall provide written verification of his/her compliance with this covenant.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the phrase 'including, but not limited to, retaining an electronic copy on any electronic device regardless of whether such device is owned by Employee' weakens the confidentiality protection. By removing the explicit prohibition against retaining electronic copies, especially on personal devices, the agreement becomes less clear.  While there's a general obligation to return confidential information, the specific prohibition is now missing. This can lead to a legal challenge if the employee does retain electronic information.
perturbation.law_explanation: The removal of the explicit prohibition against retaining electronic copies of confidential information, particularly on personal devices, could make it more difficult for the Banking Organization to obtain an injunction under the Indiana Uniform Trade Secrets Act (IUTSA) if an employee misappropriates trade secrets by retaining them electronically. The IUTSA allows for injunctions to prevent the actual or threatened misappropriation of trade secrets, but the strength of the case depends on clearly defining what constitutes misappropriation.  By weakening the language about electronic copies, the company increases the difficulty of proving that an employee's actions constitute misappropriation, contradicting the purpose of IUTSA.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Indiana General Assembly You need to enable JavaScript to run this app.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Indiana Code § 24-2-3-2. Definitions :: 2022 Indiana Code :: U.S. Codes and Statutes :: U.S. Law :: Justia There Is a Newer Version of this Section 2024 Indiana Code 2023 Indiana Code 2022 Indiana Code (here) View All Versions View Our Newest Version Here 2022 Indiana Code Title 24. Trade Regulation Article 2. Trademarks, Trade Names, and Trade Secrets Chapter 3. Trade Secrets 24-2-3-2. Definitions Universal Citation: IN Code § 24-2-3-2 (2022) Learn more This media-neutral citation is based on the American Association of Law Libraries Universal Citation Guide and is not necessarily the official citation. Previous Next Sec. 2. As used in this chapter, unless the context requires otherwise: "Improper means" includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. "Misappropriation" means: (1) acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (2) disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (A) used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; (B) at the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his knowledge of the trade secret was: (i) derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (ii) acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) derived from or through a person who owed a duty to the person seeking relief to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (C) before a material change of his position, knew or had reason to know that it was a trade secret and that knowledge of it had been acquired by accident or mistake. "Person" means a natural person, limited liability company, corporation, business trust, estate, trust, partnership, association, joint venture, government, governmental subdivision or agency, or any other legal or
perturbation.justification: The original contract text explicitly prohibited retaining electronic copies of confidential information, even on personal devices, reinforcing the protection of trade secrets. By removing this specific prohibition, the changed text weakens the confidentiality protection. Scraped Law Snippet 2 defines 'Misappropriation' under the Indiana Uniform Trade Secrets Act (IUTSA) as the 'disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent,' and 'Improper means' includes 'breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy.' While the general obligation to return confidential information remains, the explicit removal of the electronic copy prohibition makes it more difficult to prove misappropriation if an employee retains and uses such copies, as the duty to maintain secrecy regarding electronic copies is less clear. This omission directly contradicts the purpose of IUTSA by weakening the ability to prevent trade secret misappropriation, thus a contradiction exists.

perturbation.original_text: Employee agrees that a Banking Organization, or its successor or assigns, will suffer irreparable damage and injury and will not have an adequate remedy at law in the event of any breach by Employee of any provision of Sections 4, 5 or 6 , herein. Accordingly, in the event of a breach or of a threatened or attempted breach by Employee of Sections 4, 5, or 6, in addition to all other remedies to which a Banking Organization is entitled under law, in equity, or otherwise (including, but not limited to, monetary damages), a Banking Organization or its assigns and successors, shall be entitled to a temporary restraining order and/or preliminary or permanent injunction (without the necessity of showing any actual damage) or a decree of specific performance and no bond or other security shall be required in that connection.
perturbation.changed_text: Employee agrees that a Banking Organization, or its successor or assigns, will suffer irreparable damage and injury and will not have an adequate remedy at law in the event of any breach by Employee of any provision of Sections 4, 5 or 6 , herein. Accordingly, in the event of a breach or of a threatened or attempted breach by Employee of Sections 4, 5, or 6, in addition to all other remedies to which a Banking Organization is entitled under law, in equity, or otherwise (including, but not limited to, monetary damages), a Banking Organization or its assigns and successors, shall be entitled to a temporary restraining order and/or preliminary or permanent injunction or a decree of specific performance.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the phrase '(without the necessity of showing any actual damage) or no bond or other security shall be required in that connection.' increases the burden for the Banking Organization to obtain injunctive relief.  Under Indiana law, obtaining a preliminary injunction generally requires demonstrating a reasonable likelihood of success on the merits, irreparable harm, the balance of harms favors the moving party, and the public interest would not be disserved by the injunction. Removing the language suggesting no actual damage needs to be shown directly contradicts the standard for proving irreparable harm. Also the removal of the bond requirement is problematic.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Indiana Rules of Trial Procedure Rule 65
perturbation.law_citation: Indiana Rules of Trial Procedure Rule 65(C)
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification, by removing the waiver of the bond requirement, directly contradicts the typical interpretation and application of Indiana Rules of Trial Procedure, specifically Rule 65(C), which governs the issuance of injunctions. While a court ultimately decides whether to require a bond, contracts attempting to waive this requirement are viewed with scrutiny and are not always enforced. Removing the phrase essentially gives more power to the court to enforce the bond.
perturbation.justification: The change omits critical aspects related to injunctive relief, namely, '(without the necessity of showing any actual damage) or no bond or other security shall be required in that connection.' This omission increases the burden on the Banking Organization to obtain injunctive relief. The Perturbation Explanation states that the original language waives the need to demonstrate actual damage and the bond requirement. This aligns with the 'Omission Legal Contradiction' definition, as the omission removes essential information, potentially creating legal loopholes related to consumer protection by altering the requirements for injunctive relief.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be interpreted under, subject to and governed by the laws of the State of Indiana, without consideration of the choice of law principles thereof, and all questions concerning its validity, construction and administration shall be determined in accordance with Indiana law.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be interpreted under, subject to and governed by the laws of the State of Indiana, and all questions concerning its validity, construction and administration shall be determined in accordance with Indiana law.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the phrase 'without consideration of the choice of law principles thereof' the agreement introduces ambiguity and potential contradiction with Indiana law.  Indiana applies its choice-of-law rules to determine which state's law governs when there's a conflict of laws.
perturbation.law_citation: Klaxon Co. v. Stentor Elec. Mfg. Co., 313 U.S. 487 (1941)
perturbation.law_explanation: The removal of 'without consideration of the choice of law principles thereof' creates a legal contradiction because Indiana courts, following the Klaxon doctrine, are bound to apply the choice-of-law rules of Indiana. These rules dictate which jurisdiction's substantive law applies in cases involving conflicts between the laws of different states. By omitting the explicit statement that Indiana law governs without regard to choice-of-law principles, the modified text introduces uncertainty. This is because Indiana choice-of-law principles might, under certain circumstances (e.g., if the employee resides and primarily works in another state), lead to the application of another state's laws, thereby contradicting the intent to exclusively apply Indiana law.

No. 741 Argued May 1, 2, 1941 Decided June 2, 1941 313 U.S. 487 Syllabus 1. In diversity of citizenship cases, the federal courts, when deciding questions of conflict of laws, must follow the rules prevailing in the States in which they sit. Erie R. Co. v. Tompkins, 304 U. S. 64 . P. 313 U. S. 496 . 2. In an action in a federal court. in Delaware, for breach of a New York contract, the applicability of a New York statute directing that interest be added to the recovery in contract cases is a question of conflict of laws, which the federal court must determine by the law of Delaware. P. 313 U. S. 496 . 3. The Full Faith and Credit Clause does not require that a State, contrary to its own policy, shall give effect in actions brought locally on contracts made in other States, to laws of those States relating, not to the validity of such contracts, but to the right to add interest to the recovery as an incidental item of damages. Page 313 U. S. 488 § 480 N.Y.Civ.Prac. Act. John Hancock Mutual Life Inc. Co. v. Yates, 299 U. S. 178 , distinguished. P. 313 U. S. 497 . 115 F.2d 268, reversed. Certiorari, 312 U.S. 674, to review the affirmance of a judgment recovered for breach of a contract, 30 F. Supp. 425 . The review in this Court was limited to the question whether § 480 of the New York Civil Practice Act is applicable to an action in the federal court in Delaware. Page 313 U. S. 494 Read More Opinions Opinions & Dissents U.S. Supreme Court Klaxon Co. v. Stentor Elec. Mfg. Co., Inc., 313 U.S. 487 (1941) Klaxon Company v. Stentor Electric Manufacturing Co., Inc. No. 741 Argued May 1, 2, 1941 Decided June 2, 1941 313 U.S. 487 CERTIORARI TO THE CIRCUIT COURT OF APPEALS FOR THE THIRD CIRCUIT Syllabus 1. In di
perturbation.justification: The omission of 'without consideration of the choice of law principles thereof' in the changed text creates a legal contradiction because it introduces uncertainty about which state's laws apply. The Law Explanation states that Indiana courts follow the Klaxon doctrine, which necessitates the application of Indiana's choice-of-law rules. Scraped Law Snippet 2, referencing Klaxon Co. v. Stentor Elec. Mfg. Co., Inc., supports this by highlighting that federal courts in diversity of citizenship cases must follow the conflict-of-laws rules of the state in which they sit. By removing the explicit exclusion of choice-of-law principles, the modified text opens the door for the potential application of another state's laws, which contradicts the original intent to exclusively apply Indiana law. This falls under the 'Omission Legal Contradiction' definition because it removes essential information creating a legal loophole.
